title: AI, Work, and Your Career

modules.title: Automation: What History Teaches Us
modules.sub: Tracing past technological shifts to inform present fears
modules.description: Learners examine historical waves of automation—from the Industrial Revolution to computing—and identify patterns in how labor markets have responded. They weigh evidence about job destruction versus job creation to build a more calibrated starting point for the rest of the course.

modules.title: Which Jobs AI Is Changing Now
modules.sub: Mapping real disruption across industries and roles today
modules.description: Learners survey current data on which occupations and sectors are experiencing the most significant AI-driven change right now. They assess credible forecasts critically, learning to distinguish hype from documented impact so they can locate their own field on the disruption spectrum.

modules.title: Tasks Versus Jobs: A Key Distinction
modules.sub: Breaking roles into tasks to see AI's true reach
modules.description: Learners decompose familiar job titles into discrete tasks and evaluate which of those tasks AI can automate, augment, or cannot yet touch. This analytical lens helps them move beyond headline-level anxiety toward a precise understanding of where their work is actually vulnerable or durable.

modules.title: Skills That Keep You Relevant
modules.sub: Identifying durable human capabilities AI cannot easily replicate
modules.description: Learners investigate the cognitive, social, and creative skills that consistently retain value as automation advances, grounded in labor-market research. They conduct a personal skills audit and identify gaps they can address through targeted learning or repositioning.

modules.title: AI as Colleague, Not Replacement
modules.sub: Practicing productive human-AI collaboration in real workflows
modules.description: Learners explore concrete examples of professionals working alongside AI tools to achieve outcomes neither could reach alone. They design a simple collaboration workflow for their own role, practicing the mindset shift from competition with AI to strategic partnership.

modules.title: Policy Responses to Job Displacement
modules.sub: Evaluating what governments and institutions can and should do
modules.description: Learners survey a range of policy proposals—retraining programs, portable benefits, universal basic income, and sector-specific regulation—and analyze the evidence for their effectiveness. They form and defend a reasoned position on which interventions are most promising given structural labor-market realities.

modules.title: Designing a Career for the AI Era
modules.sub: Building a personal roadmap that anticipates ongoing change
modules.description: Learners apply course insights to craft an actionable career development plan tailored to an AI-reshaping landscape. They set learning priorities, identify pivot opportunities, and build contingency thinking into their plan so it remains useful even as circumstances shift.

modules.title: Community and Collective Adaptation
modules.sub: Looking beyond individuals to shared resilience and mutual support
modules.description: Learners examine how communities, professional networks, unions, and civic organizations are collectively responding to AI-driven labor disruption. They identify one collective structure they can participate in or strengthen, leaving the course with both a personal strategy and a sense of shared agency.

synopsis: This course moves beyond fear and hype to give workers, students, and managers a realistic picture of how AI is reshaping labor markets and what individuals can do about it. Learners will leave with practical strategies and a clearer sense of the structural forces at play.
rationale: With a demand score of 6/10 and a corpus similarity of 0.61 to 'What AI Actually Does to Jobs', there is space for a more holistic, action-oriented course that covers economic policy, career strategy, and collective responses rather than impact description alone.
nearest_existing: What AI Actually Does to Jobs
